question_en: For a vector $V$ of $n$ nonnegative integers whose maximum value is $m$, which statements about sorting are correct? I. QuickSort and MergeSort take $\Omega(n \log n)$ time to sort $V$. II. If $m = O(n)$, $V$ can be sorted in $O(n)$ worst-case time. III. QuickSort has $O(n \log n)$ worst-case time when $m = O(n)$. IV. If $n = O(m)$, Counting Sort is more efficient than MergeSort as a function of $n$.

choices.A: Only I and II are correct.
choices.B: Only I and III are correct.
choices.C: Only II and IV are correct.
choices.D: Only III and IV are correct.

correct_answer_en: Only I and II are correct.
explanation_en: Comparison sorting has an $\Omega(n \log n)$ lower bound, while Counting Sort is linear when the key range $m$ is $O(n)$. QuickSort still has quadratic worst-case behavior, so III is false.

question_en: An algorithm repeatedly selects the smallest item in the unsorted part of an array and swaps it into the first remaining position. Which sorting method is being described?
explanation_en: Selection sort repeatedly selects the minimum remaining element and places it in its final sorted position.

question_en: Which statements about choosing sorting algorithms are correct? I. HeapSort is appropriate when worst-case behavior matters. II. Insertion Sort is appropriate when the array is almost sorted. III. QuickSort is usually good for average-case performance. IV. Bubble Sort is ideal when best and worst case must have the same complexity.

choices.A: Only I and II are correct.
choices.B: Only II and III are correct.
choices.C: Only I and IV are correct.
choices.D: Only I, II, and III are correct.

correct_answer_en: Only I, II, and III are correct.
explanation_en: HeapSort has $O(n \log n)$ worst-case time, Insertion Sort performs well on nearly sorted data, and QuickSort is strong on average. Bubble Sort is not ideal for equal best/worst complexity.

choices.A: Repeatedly inserts each element into a sorted prefix.
choices.B: Builds a heap and repeatedly extracts the maximum element.
choices.C: Splits the input into halves, sorts them, and merges the results.
choices.D: Chooses a pivot, partitions elements around it, and recursively sorts the partitions.
choices.E: Counts the number of elements with each key value.

correct_answer_en: Chooses a pivot, partitions elements around it, and recursively sorts the partitions.
explanation_en: QuickSort is defined by pivot selection, partitioning into smaller/larger groups, and recursive sorting of those groups.

question_en: What is the asymptotic time complexity of MergeSort on an input of size $n$?
explanation_en: MergeSort solves two subproblems of size $n/2$ and merges in linear time, giving $T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n)=O(n \log n)$.

question_en: MergeSort, Kruskal's minimum spanning tree algorithm, and Floyd-Warshall are examples of which algorithmic paradigms, respectively?

choices.A: Greedy, dynamic programming, divide and conquer
choices.B: Divide and conquer, dynamic programming, greedy
choices.C: Greedy, divide and conquer, dynamic programming
choices.D: Dynamic programming, divide and conquer, greedy
choices.E: Divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming

correct_answer_en: Divide and conquer, greedy, dynamic programming
explanation_en: MergeSort divides and combines subproblems, Kruskal greedily adds safe edges, and Floyd-Warshall uses dynamic programming over intermediate vertices.
